body_txt: When fighting, two or more animated or comic strip characters may vanish into a ball of dust or smoke, from which the sounds of a really violent struggle emanate. Hands, fists, feet, and other random objects may protrude momentarily, then be pulled back in, as well as hands wielding weapons (usually the blunt-force-trauma kind). Often, swirls, five-pointed stars, and other objects can be seen emerging from the ball. This is used to indicate that the fight is so violent that:  During a really long fight, the Big Ball of Violence may start rolling around the area, sucking up any nearby unsuspecting or curious bystanders or objects into the fight (not unlike other big stickyballs ) — one of which is usually That Poor Cat that was in the vicinity. Sometimes a shoe flies out for comedic effect. Sometimes a character escapes from the Big Ball of Violence, leaving the other participant to realize he's been beating up himself (or, if several characters had ganged up on the escapee, each other), or if a "reluctant" participant tries to sneak away and escape immediately gets dragged back into it. Or a character may be thrown out of the ball, angrily get up and jump back into the fray. In other cases, if a character witnesses a Second-Person Attack , and is easily overpowered, then the Big Ball Of Violence may mean the overpowered character is taking an ass-kicking. Another common gag is to have the fight come to a sudden halt, usually at the demands of an authority figure, with the participants stuck in ridiculous poses mid-attack, such as trying to bite somebody in the leg, or about to use a lethal weapon they certainly didn't have going into it. In some media (notably video games), a Big Ball of Violence can be used as a shorthand for any sort of activity that requires a lot of physical strength and energy, and isn't specific to just fighting. It's not uncommon for a character building something very fast to disappear behind a "Big Ball of Construction", and come out with some sort of high-quality building afterwards. A rarely-used cloudless variant exists, with the fight being depicted as a literal Big Ball of Violence with various moments of the fight confined into a whirling, circular shape. Take Our Word for It that Battle Discretion Shot serves a similar function. A Sub-Trope of Fight Unscene . It can also be a sub-trope of Nothing Is Funnier , if it is played for comedic effect. For instance, when the smoke clears, someone in the party may be liable to be sporting some Amusing Injuries . Compare Thundering Herd .
